unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Bring together your strongest vocabulary, reading, grammar, speaking and writing from Units 1-9. Create a polished English product for a real audience, present it clearly, respond to questions and reflect on your Grade 3 growth.
unit.learningPath: Choose evidence. Review language. Plan and draft. Improve from feedback. Present. Reflect. Complete the final course quiz.

visual.alt: A Grade 3 learner presenting a completed English portfolio

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The people who live with you and love you.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My family has my mum, my dad, my sister, and me.

The word family belongs to our me and my family vocabulary.

readings.title: Review text 1: Musa's Big Day
readings.theme: All About Me and My Family
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Musa was not his real name. His real name was Abdullahi. But everyone at school called him Musa, because his father was a teacher and everyone gave the family great respect.
At home, Musa lived with his big, happy family. His parents, his two sisters, and his grandmother all lived together in a small house near the town centre.
One morning, Musa was getting ready for school. His mother said, “Musa, remember to behave in public. Greet your teacher and listen well.”
“I will, Mama,” said Musa. “I am so excited! Today our class is doing a play about health and safety.”
His little sister Samira asked, “What is the play about?”
“It is about staying safe at school and showing respect,” Musa explained.
At school, the students were busy. Some were practising their lines. Some were fixing the props with paper and paint. Musa put on a yellow vest and a hat he had made from old newspaper.
He whispered to his best friend, Adam, “What is your duty in the play?”
“I am playing the senior student,” said Adam. “You have to teach me how to act in public and private places.”
“Oh yes!” said Musa. “That is my job!”
The teacher clapped her hands. “Alright, everyone. We are starting now.” The classroom became quiet, and Musa stepped forward.
“Hello, Senior Student!” he said in a big voice. “This is how you behave in the lunch area. Sit down, eat nicely, and speak softly.”
“Why should I do that?” asked Adam, pretending to be rude.
Musa replied, “Because we must show honour and respect. Behave well, and keep other people safe.”
Then another student walked in and shouted loudly. Musa smiled and said, “In private, you can be loud with your family. But in public, we stay calm.” The whole class laughed. They loved the play.
After the play, the teacher smiled. “Who are your role models?” she asked the class.
Musa raised his hand. “My parents,” he said. “They teach me to help others and to be polite. I want to honour them.”
The teacher nodded. “That is a wonderful answer, Musa.”
Later that day, Musa saw a junior student drop her school bag near the gate. Her books spilled onto the ground.
He ran over at once. “Let me help you,” he said kindly.
“Thank you!” said the girl, smiling. “You are very respectful.”  Musa smiled back. “I am trying to be.”
On the way home, Musa and Adam walked together. “I liked your line in the play,” said Adam. “‘Behave well, and keep others safe.’ That was clever.”
Musa grinned. “We should make posters for school,” he said. “Like ‘Be safe in public’ or ‘Use kind words.’”
“That is a great idea,” said Adam. “Let us ask the teacher tomorrow.”
That evening at home, Musa was writing in his notebook. His grandmother came in. “What are you doing?” she asked.
“I am writing about my duty,” he said. “To help others and to be a good student.” He showed her what he had written:
“At school, I behave with respect. In public, I speak softly. In private, I relax with my family. My duty is to learn, to help, and to stay healthy.”
His mother peeked into the room. “Time for bed,” she said gently.
“Okay, Mama,” said Musa. “Tomorrow I will keep being my best.”
“Good,” she said. “Because every day is a new chance to do your duty.”
Musa closed his eyes and smiled. He liked his nickname now. It reminded him that he was still learning, but growing up with honour. And maybe one day, he would be a senior student that others could look up to.

readings.title: Review text 2: The Grammar Champions
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Samira sat quietly in her classroom at Kisii Central Primary School. She held her pencil in one hand and her eraser in the other. Her teacher had just said something exciting.
“Next Friday,” said Mr. Musa, “we will have a grammar contest!”
The whole class cheered.
“You will work in groups,” he explained. “You will read, write, and even perform a short lesson. Start to prepare today!”
Samira joined her group with her friends Ibrahim and Nadia. Their group name was “The Grammar Champions.”
“We need to choose a topic,” said Ibrahim.
“Let's pick something from the grammar book,” said Nadia.
“What about prepositions?” asked Samira. “We can show how to use small words like ‘in’, ‘on’, and ‘under’.”
“I like it,” said Ibrahim. “It is simple, but important.”
The next day, the group met in the library. They wanted to study together and write their report.
Nadia opened a book and smiled. “This author is funny,” she said. “He explains grammar with cartoons!”
Samira's eyes lit up. “That gives me an idea,” she said. “We can draw pictures too.”
Ibrahim brought a notebook full of details. “These are notes from my sister's class,” he said. “She already graduated from primary school.”
“She must be clever,” said Nadia.
“She is,” said Ibrahim. “She loves education.”
For the next few days, the group worked hard. They practised their lesson in front of the class. They wrote their report about grammar rules. They checked every spelling word twice. They even collected classroom supplies like coloured chalk, tape, and posters.
Finally, it was contest day. The classroom had bright decorations and rows of chairs for visitors.
One of the judges asked, “Did you study well?”
“Yes, we did!” said Samira.
Another judge smiled. “What did you learn in mathematics today?”
“About shapes,” said Ibrahim. “But grammar is our focus today!”
When it was their turn, the Grammar Champions stood up tall.
Ibrahim began. “This topic is from our grammar book.”
Nadia added, “We will show how to use the words ‘in’, ‘on’, and ‘under’.”
Samira stepped forward and pointed to the board. “The teacher's pen is ‘on’ the desk,” she said. “The bag is ‘under’ the table.”
Ibrahim drew a funny cartoon on the board. Everyone laughed and clapped.
After the show, the judges talked quietly. Then Mr. Musa stood up and announced, “The winners are… The Grammar Champions!”
The class cheered again.
“We won!” whispered Samira. “All that preparing helped us.”
Later, the teacher asked, “Where did the contest happen?”
“In our own classroom,” Nadia answered.
“And why did we win?” asked Ibrahim.
“Because we worked as a team,” said Samira. “And we gave a clear lesson with good details.”
At home, Samira told her mother the good news. “I feel so proud,” she said. “I want to learn even more grammar.”
Her mother hugged her. “That,” she said, “is the spirit of education.”
The next morning, Samira wrote in her journal:
I studied my grammar, I wrote a report,
I used my eraser to fix and to sort.
In the library, I read with my friends,
And now I have learned how good learning ends —
With joy, and with help, and a clever plan.
We're Grammar Champions. Yes, we can!

readings.title: Review text 3: The Calendar on the Wall
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
One bright Monday morning, Sarah sat in class and looked at the big calendar on the wall. Her teacher had just asked a question.
“When does the school year begin?”
“In January,” said Sarah.
“Good,” said the teacher. “And when does it end?”
“In November,” said another student.
“Correct. Now let us each make a calendar that shows what we do each month.”
Sarah liked the idea. She wrote her name at the top of her paper and drew a square for each month: January, February, March, and so on.
Then she thought about the future.
“In June, we have school games,” she wrote. “In August, we go on vacation to visit Grandma.”
That night, at home, she showed the calendar to her grandmother.
“In October, we pick mangoes,” said Grandma, smiling. “And in December, the weather is hot.”
“What happened in the old days, Grandma?” asked Sarah.
“Oh, a lot,” she said. “A century ago, people did not have printed calendars. They watched the sky, the moon, and the trees to know the time of year.”
Sarah’s eyes grew wide. “A hundred years ago?”
“Yes,” Grandma laughed. “A century sounds long, but it goes faster than you think.”
The next day at school, the teacher asked, “Did you prepare your calendar?”
“Yes,” said Sarah. “And I asked my Grandma about the past.”
“Great,” said the teacher. “Now add some details. What happens each hour of your day?”
Sarah wrote: “At 6 o’clock, I wake up. At 7, I eat breakfast. At 8, I walk to school. At 1, we eat lunch. At 4, I go home and help with the chores.”
Then she thought about the future again. “When I grow up,” she wrote, “I want to be a teacher. I will help students plan their day and prepare for the next century.”
Later in the week, the school had a quiz.
“What month comes sooner — April or May?” asked the teacher.
“April,” said Sarah. “It comes sooner.”
“And what will happen soon?” asked the teacher.
“Our vacation!” the class shouted together.
At the end of the week, parents visited the school. Sarah showed her calendar to her mother.
“Wow,” said her mother. “You even marked the days we plant maize.”
Sarah smiled. “I want to remember everything.”
Her mother added, “That is smart. The calendar helps us plan.”
On the last page of her book, Sarah wrote a short poem:
The months go fast, the hours too.
The future is coming, it’s all brand new.
Look at the past, remember it well,
Time is a story we all must tell.

readings.title: Review text 4: From Our Village to the County
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Amal lived in a small village near Mount Meru in northern Tanzania. The village was quiet, with tall trees, green farms, and goats walking slowly along the road.
Every Friday, Amal went with her class to learn about the community. One morning, their teacher said, “Today, we will visit the county center.” The whole class clapped. Amal had never been there before.
They climbed into a big school bus and set off. “Look out of the window,” said the teacher. “What can you see?”
“I can see the market,” said Musa. “There are a lot of people!”
“I can see a garden,” said Sophia. “It has flowers and trees.”
Their first stop was the hospital. A kind nurse met them at the door. “This is a public hospital,” she said. “Many people visit here every day.”
Selma raised her hand. “Who works here?” she asked.
The nurse smiled. “The doctor works here. He helps people who are sick.” The class walked through the hall and passed clean rooms with beds, medicine, and smiling workers.
“Exit the hospital quietly,” said the teacher. “People are resting.”
Next, they walked to the court. “What happens here?” asked Sophia.
“This is where problems are solved fairly,” said the teacher. “An officer and a judge work here.” The building was large and painted white. A sign read: “Court — County of Arusha.”
Inside, they sat quietly on the wooden benches. “Can you see the big chair?” asked the teacher. “That is where the judge sits.”
After the court, they walked to the market. Sellers called out beside baskets of bananas, maize, and rice. “This place is busy,” said Amal. “Our village market is much smaller.”
“Some markets are small,” said the teacher, “and others have a lot of things.” They bought sweet mangoes and sat near a small garden to eat.
“Next is the college,” said the teacher.
“What is a college?” asked Musa.
“It is a school for older students,” said the teacher. “There, they learn to be nurses, builders, or officers.”
At the college gate, a friendly student greeted them. “My name is Isaac,” he said. “I study health and safety.”
“Do you want to be a doctor?” asked Sophia.
“Yes,” said Isaac, “because I want to help my village.”
As they walked back to the bus, Amal saw a sign by the road. “What is the border?” she asked.
“It is where one county ends and another begins,” said the teacher. “That is the border between Arusha and Kilimanjaro counties.”
When they returned to school, the teacher said, “Now write about what you saw.” Amal picked up her pencil and wrote:
“Today we went from our village to the county center. We saw a hospital where the doctor works. We visited the court, the college, the market, and a garden. I learned that many people work to help us. I like the officer because he protects us. One day I want to be a sailor and see more places — but I will always come home.”
The teacher smiled at her paper. “This is a good report,” she said. “It has details, and it shows what you learned.”
That evening, Amal shared her writing with her grandmother. “Very nice,” said Grandma. “Your address may say ‘village,’ but your ideas travel far.”
Selma laughed. “Maybe one day I will see the sea, meet a real sailor, or even cross a border,” she said.
Her grandmother nodded gently. “Yes. But never forget your village roots.”

readings.title: Review text 5: The Wall Behind the Garden
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
It was the first week of school in a small village. The rain had stopped, and the fields were dry and warm. The children helped sweep the schoolyard and water the garden behind the classrooms.
That morning, the teacher smiled and said, “Today we will start a special project. Together, we will build a new reading wall in the garden.”
“What will be on the wall?” asked Nadia.
“Stories, poems, and pictures,” said the teacher. “But first, we must remove the old, broken fence.”
The class went outside. Some children pulled weeds. Others removed old wires and sticks. Then they saw something strange — a hole in the ground, covered by stones and dry leaves.
“What happened here?” asked Noah, looking closely.
“I don't know,” said Nadia. “Let's discuss it with the teacher.”
The teacher looked at the hole and said, “A long time ago, this was an animal path. Goats used to escape from this yard right here.”
The children laughed. “We don't want goats in our classroom!”
“Then we must block the path well,” said the teacher. “Let's plan carefully and build a strong wall.”
First, they searched for smooth, flat stones. Then, they discussed the design and the colours. Finally, they began to build.
Nadia painted birds. Noah drew a blue waterfall. Some children wrote short poems.
But suddenly the clouds turned dark. The wind blew hard, and the wall began to shake.
“Hold the frame!” shouted the teacher. Nadia ran to help. Together they held the pieces until the wind slowed down.
“We need to fix it,” said Noah. “We didn't complete the support.”
“I think we should add more stones,” said Nadia.
“I don't think paint alone is strong enough to hold it,” said another child.
The next day, everyone came early. “We will finish today,” said the teacher. “Let's be strong and work together.” They completed the wall and made it much stronger.
The final touch was a sign that read: “We built this wall to share words and protect dreams.”
Later that week, the whole village came to celebrate. There was singing, clapping, and food from every family.
“This is a true celebration,” said the teacher. “You all helped. You worked hard.”
“Nadia's drawing is my favourite,” said the principal.
“And Noah's poem,” said the teacher. “He wrote about how we protect our stories and our voices.”
After the event, Nadia saw a younger girl standing near the wall.
“Can I draw here one day?” the girl asked shyly.
“Of course,” said Nadia kindly. “This wall is for everyone.”
That night, Nadia wrote in her notebook: “We built something beautiful. It was windy and messy. But we listened to each other. We shared our ideas. We offered help. And we finished something strong.”
The next morning, a visitor came to the school. He had travelled from a nearby town.
“I work in education,” he said. “I heard you made something special.” The students proudly gave him a tour of the wall.
“You are helping this place develop,” he said. “This wall is not just a wall. It is a gift.”
When he left, Noah asked, “Why did he call it a gift?”
“Because it gives people ideas,” said the teacher. “And new ideas are part of how a village grows and develops.”
Nadia nodded and smiled. “And we made it,” she said. “Together.”

grammar.title: Sentence accuracy audit
grammar.explanation: Review agreement, tense and complete sentences.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Every sentence needs a clear subject and verb.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.
grammar.practice: Correct six sentences from your portfolio.

grammar.title: Tense control
grammar.explanation: Check that past, present and future time are clear.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Yesterday I visited. Today I learn. Tomorrow I will present.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.
grammar.practice: Revise six verbs and explain each tense choice.

grammar.title: Questions and responses
grammar.explanation: Use accurate Wh- and yes/no questions.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Where did you learn this? Why is it important?
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.
grammar.practice: Write and answer six audience questions.

grammar.title: Description and precision
grammar.explanation: Improve nouns, verbs, adjectives and adverbs.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: The thoughtful learner explained the idea clearly.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.

grammar.title: Joining ideas
grammar.explanation: Connect ideas with and, but, because, so and when.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: I revised my story because feedback helped me.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.

grammar.title: Final editing conference
grammar.explanation: Apply capitals, spelling, punctuation and paragraphing.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Read, mark, revise, then read again.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.

writing.support: Use a planning frame, word bank and one model from an earlier unit.
writing.extension: Add a second paragraph, varied sentence openings and a purposeful conclusion.

quizzes.question: What does 'family' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who learns in school. | A person you like and spend time with. | A younger person, or someone in a lower class. | The people who live with you and love you.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The people who live with you and love you.
quizzes.explanation: My family has my mum, my dad, my sister, and me.

quizzes.question: What does 'parent' mean?
quizzes.options: A mother or a father. | A person who learns in school. | A younger person, or someone in a lower class. | An older person, or someone who has learned more.
quizzes.explanation: My parents take me to school every morning.

quizzes.question: What does 'teacher' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who helps us learn new things. | A place where you can find and borrow books. | The subject where we work with numbers, shapes, and measuring. | To finish school or a class.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A person who helps us learn new things.
quizzes.explanation: My teacher helps me with my mathematics homework.

quizzes.question: What does 'author' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who writes books or stories. | The rules for how we put words together to speak and write. | The subject where we work with numbers, shapes, and measuring. | To finish school or a class.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A person who writes books or stories.
quizzes.explanation: The author wrote a funny book about a talking camel.

The people who live with you and love you. My family has my mum, my dad, my sister, and me.
A mother or a father. My parents take me to school every morning.
A person who helps us learn new things. My teacher helps me with my mathematics homework.
A person who writes books or stories. The author wrote a funny book about a talking camel.
The first month of the year. The school year begins in January.
The second month of the year, usually with 28 days. February is the shortest month.
A person who helps sick people feel better. The doctor works at the hospital.
A person who helps keep people safe and makes sure rules are followed. The officer helped us cross the road.
To talk about something with other people. We will discuss our ideas in a group.
To use your ears to hear carefully. Please listen to the teacher when she talks.
